sentences.text: Oh, I see.
I shouldn't touch.
I see.
So I went the next day.
And when I came to the gestapo, there was a big sign, no Jews, entry forbidden.
But I went through.
And I saw a nice fat guy.
And I always have the idea fat guys are a lot easier to handle.
And I spoke to him in Viennese, in a very Viennese dialect.
And I said, I came to see [INAUDIBLE].
I came extra from Vienna.
And he thought, in his stupid mind, I was sent from Vienna to the Geheime Staatspolizei to see this man.
So he said, all right.
We are nearly [INAUDIBLE].
We are nearly-- I am from Munich.
Munich and Vienna, that's really very near.
So I take you to him.
So he took me to him.
And this guy, he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], and offered me a seat.
What is on your mind?
And I told him.
And you see, in some way, I was really innocent.
I didn't still believe it Nazi mentality.
And I said, you see, Germany is [GERMAN].
I don't think they care for justice.
I don't think you keep a man who is innocent in jail.
My husband had no love affair with a gentile girl.
Yeah?
He paid his taxes.
And then he said, I think he even didn't have a parking ticket.
And it was really stupid.
But anyways, this guy started to laugh.
Yeah?
And he said, now, I tell you.
I'll ask for his papers from Vienna, and you come in two days, and I give you an answer.
Well, I-- I thank him very much.
After two days, I went there.
And as he said, whatever you told me is true.
I can only confirm it.
And what I can do to help you, I would do.
Come tomorrow.
And so I came for a whole week every day.
And he always said, come tomorrow.
And this cousin of my husband, he said, I told you.
It's hopeless.
Yeah?
Don't believe it.
Perhaps he like conversation with you, yeah?
I said no, I trust him.
I have a good feeling with this guy.
But I lost my nerves, and I made a very crazy thing.
I made a phone call to the [INAUDIBLE] in Geneva.
And the news man, he was a [GERMAN] my husband, a Dr.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I said, send me a fake telegram.
Certificate only valid to this and this date.
Let's see, three or four days, yeah?
[INAUDIBLE] a little bit more.
Perhaps one and a half week, yeah?
And this idea could have been for my family death or concentration camp, sent me a fake telegram.
Yeah?
I took-- I got this telegram, and I went to my friend in the gestapo.
And I said to him, you see, even in this bad times, I have a possibility to speak with a human being like you.
But the British people are bureaucrats, you see.
My visa is canceled when I don't come.
He said, do you know?
The whole act of your husband is finished.
I need only is the signature of [INAUDIBLE], and this I get today.
I would advise you-- today is Friday, yeah?
Take the train to Munich, the night train.
Yeah?
And you will get your husband tomorrow.
In the [INAUDIBLE] the Geheime Staatspolizei.
I thanked him very much and I left.
And I told [INAUDIBLE] my cousin-- this man from the [INAUDIBLE].
He said, I can't explain how sorry I am for you.
This guy makes fun of you.
What are you doing?
I said, I take the train and do it.
He said, I tell you what.
I have a friend and attorney.
He will go with you in Munich.
I tell him to go to the station and pick you up.
How will you recognize me?
I said.
I described your size and how you look, what will you wear.
I said, a blue suit, blue head.
All right.
So I arrived in Munich, and this guy was already here.
And he said, I have spoke with Alfred.
I go with you to the Geheime Staatspolizei.
I don't believe one minute you'll get your husband.
But we do it.
So we went there.
He went with me.
And he said [SPEAKING GERMAN].
We sent him to Buchenwald today.
Buchenwald is another concentration camp near Weimar.
So you see-- you see, he said to me.
What will you do now?
I said, I call then my guy in Berlin.
He said, how could you dare, a Jewish woman, to call a man in the Geheime Staatspolizei?
I said, I have to do it.
I feel.
It's a force in me.
I can't go home, yeah?
So I called, and this man was on the phone.
And he said, I waited your call.
Because you think I make fun of you, huh?
No.
You take a night train to Weimar, and your husband will be free tomorrow from Buchenwald.
The attorney was hearing the conversation.
He said, he sounds sincere, but I have to tell you another thing.
First is [GERMAN].
Second, tomorrow is Sunday.
Have you ever heard that something happens in Germany on a Sunday?
Except church service.
So I said, anyway, I have to go.
So he took me to his station.
And his wife and [INAUDIBLE], I remember I took [INAUDIBLE] only.
They put chocolates on my pillow case for a sweet sleep.
And I arrived in Weimar at 7 o'clock in the morning.
And I took a cab to the Geheime Staatspolizei.
And [INAUDIBLE] they're very friendly.
I felt already better.
I had a feeling they thought that the imprisonment of my husband was an error or something.
Because the guy said, sit down, have a cup of coffee.
It's too early to go to Buchenwald, yeah?
You can't go before 8 o'clock.
So at 8 o'clock, I took a cab.
And I came to Buchenwald.
And I came-- I tell you, the drive was the most beautiful thing.
Because the landscape is so beautiful.
And it was autumn, and the trees were in all colors of red.
And even in my mood, I saw it.
And I said, one has to-- this has to be painted, how it looks.
And it looks as the forest grew darker and darker.
And then we came to the camp, and then I saw my husband.
And he looked awful.
His suit was torn.
His eye was swollen.
His nose was swollen.
And his suit was bloody and torn.
And I had to sign a receipt, said I got him alive.
And this I signed.
And I took him to Weimar.
In the first hotel, I made him a bath and ordered him a meal and said, listen.
Perhaps you lay down, and I'll go out and buy you a suit.
You'll feel better.
And don't tell me.
I don't want to hear anything.
And he said, I can't speak anyway.
He was in an awful state.
So I went out and bought him a suit.
And when I came back, what are we doing now?
I said, now you have to go out of Germany immediately.
And I will see that you go to Geneva, yeah?
And I shall go to Vienna and say how I can liquidate and take [?
Helga. ?] And we meet in Geneva, and we go together.
I have already a booking on a boat.
Do you have your passport?
No.
Where is your passport?
My passport is still in Dachau.
That was a very serious handicap.
Very serious.
So I said, all right.
We go to Munich.
I called [INAUDIBLE], who was so nice to go with me.
And he took my husband in his house.
And he took my husband-- it was Rosh Hashanah.
He took my husband to the synagogue.
And he gave him all the honors, you see.
I'm not really-- I don't know enough to tell you what he had to say, but he took him to the Torah.
And he said, a miracle has happened.
A living man came out of the depths of hell, and we have to give him all the honors we can give.
And there is something special.
I really don't know what he did.
Reading the Torah, I would say a prayer.
I can't tell you, because I really-- I have not the name, and I don't know what was it.
Anyway, my husband stayed in the home of this attorney, and I was traveling to Munich to go to Dachau for his passport.
Because without a passport, there was nothing to do.
So I went to Munich, and I went to Dachau and asked for his passport.
And they put me in some observation tower to wait.
And I tell you, I was in such a mood.
I turned my back to the window.
I didn't want to see-- I didn't want to see anything.
And after two hours, they came and brought me.
It was the worst two hours in my life.
They brought me the passport.
And still-- this is German order-- I think seven shillings that he had still on his account.
So I took her passport and went back to Munich.
And I said to his attorney, to the attorney, I even would like to take him tonight out of Germany.
Yeah?
And [INAUDIBLE] go back to Vienna.
And we decided the best thing would be to go to Basel.
[INAUDIBLE].
Because [INAUDIBLE] has, in the railway station, two exits.
One is Switzerland, and one is Germany.
Have you heard about this?
So I took him to [INAUDIBLE].
And I still remember he went-- when we were on the streets, he went along the walls of the buildings.
You see?
He was so scared.
And I brought him to the [INAUDIBLE], and they took care of him and put him in a hotel, and I went back to Vienna.
And I had already advertised for the house to sell.
And there was one man and his wife who fell in love with the house immediately.
And I don't remember how much I asked for the house, let's say 100,000 shilling.
I don't remember.
And he was not bargaining with me.
He said it is exactly the sum.
He said, I got this dowry from my wife, and I wanted to invest, and it's perfect, and we fell in love with the house.
So in this time, to sell, it has to go to a certain office-- it was called [GERMAN]-- to makes a transfer of Jewish property to some Arian buyer.
This guy, he wasn't blacklisted, but he was a little bit suspicious, because he was a monarchist.
You know?
This means, more or less, an enemy of the Nazis.
So anyway, we went both together to this office.
And they said, yes, yes, we approve of the sale.
[INAUDIBLE] we are really glad to hear it, yeah?
But I tell you, this Jewish woman gets 30% less from the price you agreed.
So we went down the stairs.
We both signed.
And we went down the stairs.
And his name was [INAUDIBLE].
So he said, when we were going down, now you think [INAUDIBLE] swine, these pigs.
He arranged it nicely, that you get less.
But I tell you before what I told you, I give you.
And I give this 30% black under the table.
So you have some black money.
That was, for this man, very dangerous.
And for me, very dangerous to accept and for him to give.
Anyway, two days later he got a letter.
The sale of the house is finally approved, and the price is $130,000.
That mean $30,000 more than he agreed first.
So there was no idea that he give me anything, because he had to make a loan from his father-in-law for the $30,000.
They didn't have the money.
Anyway, he got the house.
And this man did everything, what he could, to be helpful, risking his life.
He was driving me around the last errands, and everything you see.
Because I got permission to take the furniture that was very costly, and the carpets, out of the country.
No money.
I got an allowance to get three-- the value of three English pounds, approximately, cash.
Yeah?
And I could pay-- from the money of the building, I could pay the travel expense.
That was it.
And he was driving me around.
[INAUDIBLE] a lot of errands to do.
And then he brought me to the station.
And went with my daughter to go out, to leave my homeland.
I cried a little bit.
He cried.
And I said, really, [INAUDIBLE], I don't know how to thank you how you behaved.
And he said, I can tell you what you can do.
When you are out of this hell, you say there are still honest people in Austria.
And tell them you met an honest man.
And that was my last word with him.
And in connection with this conversation, years, years later, after the war, you had an opportunity to make sales under pressure, not valid, and get everything back.
[INAUDIBLE], I have not got money for my house.
What I got was-- I would say a tip, yeah?
So I wrote to him.
And I said, Harry [INAUDIBLE], you can sleep quiet.
I don't ask my house back.
And that is my thank you, how you behaved.
And I said, keep this letter.
That is very important for you, even for [INAUDIBLE].
And he did.
That was years, years later.
And when I came to Vienna later-- I came to Vienna the first time, I guess, '51.
With this gentlemen, and I left.
And I met my husband.
I went to Switzerland, and I met my husband.
And from there, we went to [INAUDIBLE], and we took the boat to Palestine.
Sukkot, that you mean how quick it was.
Because I told you, Rosh Hashanah was really his life saving journey.
In Sukkot, we arrived in Israel.
'38, yeah.
We arrived in Jaffa, and there was his brother, and two brothers, and family, and his mother.
And we were all very happy.
My husband was still in a situation, he couldn't have one real serious thought, one reaction to reality.
He really couldn't believe he's free.
So anyway, we came to Palestine, and now came the news problem.
What are we doing?
He had a very religious brother who took us in the first days.
And ultra orthodox.
And you could imagine, I had no idea from everything, when he was yelling at me, that I put electricity on Friday night to clean my teeth.
He was yelling.
And I wear shorts, of course.
It was awful hot.
And I had the same figure I have today.
I have always the same weight.
I was slim, and I was wearing shorts.
And he said to my husband, no Jewish woman should wear this shameless outfit of your wife!
Anyway, he was looking, you see, and I had a feeling he enjoyed what he sees.
But he said, no Jewish woman should do it.
So I spoke with my mother-in-law.
She adored me.
She was my guest in Vienna a few times, and she has always told me, one hair of you is more valuable to me than all my children.
She really loved me.
So I said, oh my [?
Clara, ?] what are we doing?
I can't stay here, you know he drives me crazy.
I can't.
And my child couldn't understand his yelling all about, because he has no idea either.
So anyway, she said, I have an idea.
In Vienna, you were knitting so beautifully, yeah?
Here is wool, the most beautiful wool, and no one knows how to do it.
I have spoken with a Russian woman who, she's [INAUDIBLE] with her housekeeping, and she would invest something and make with you a knitting parlor.
And you will give instructions and finish the things, and she gives you a guarantee for living.
So I met this woman.
The difficulty was I didn't speak one Hebrew-- what Hebrew and not Russian-- and our common language was French.
So we communicated, and she said she will invest 20 Palestinian pounds.
And she offers me a guarantee of six pounds a month.
With six pounds, I could have my rent paid and what I need living expenses.
And I insisted even-- that was my European background-- I don't want this little girl playing on the streets like all the girls.
I need someone to take care of her.
What you will call a kinder menschen.
So we arranged this, and I found an apartment in the best neighborhood of Tel Aviv, opposite today Grand Hotel [INAUDIBLE], the best neighborhood.
And I practically was a perfectionist, and I wanted only the best.
And this had a beautiful terrace, and I thought, here the women will knit.
And it worked perfectly well.
It was a big success.
The problem, how will you explain the woman what to do?
You are one week here.
You don't speak Hebrew, of course.
So I asked this French woman to tell me exactly what I need-- to deduct stitches, to add stitches, the colors and the numbers.
And all this, I put down in writing and learned it like a parrot.
You see?
And when these women came for instructions, and I spoke to them and told them in Hebrew, they said, this woman is a miracle.
She's one week here, and she speaks Hebrew, and these people were very proud Zionists, you see, in Palestine.
Then they would ask me what day is today, or what's your name?
I wouldn't give them an answer.
I wouldn't know!
But as a parrot, it worked perfectly well.
Years, years later, when I was already wealthy, I learned Hebrew, because I was fascinated with the language.
But this worked perfectly well a couple of years.
But I was, in all this time, only half a person.
Because my mother was still in Vienna.
And I knew an old woman-- you see, today, 72 is not old.
I'm 84, and I don't feel old.
But in this time, over 70, you're a very old woman, and she was sickly.
And I spoke with everyone.
And this cousin, who arranged the certificate for us, was married with a Sabra.
A Sabra is a woman born in Israel that comes-- when I compare it, like Mayflower, Americans.
Yeah?
And she knew a lot of people, and her cousin was [INAUDIBLE].
He was late foreign minister in Israel, yeah?
And she introduced me.
And he said, I tell you.
There's only one person who can help.
That is the high Commissioner.
And he doesn't want to see any people.
He's a very strange man.
But a friend of mine is playing bridge with him, and I take you to-- he will take you to Jerusalem and introduce you.
So he did it, and I saw the high commissioner, and he was very friendly.
And he looked at me very, I can say politely and interested.
And I told him, I want my mother.
He said, were you not informed?
There are no parent certificates.
No.
Decidedly not two years.
Imagine, it was before the war.
One thought the war can break out every day.
So what should I say?
He said, I'm very sorry, ma'am.
There is no-- and he was looking me over, you see.
And I was sitting with crossed legs.
And I had very good legs, and I always knew how to use them.
But I was, in this time, not in a flirtatious mood.
But I felt his eyes on my legs, you see.
And he said, with a very ugly smile, you know, we are not interested really in putting old ladies.
And I was jumping up.
I'm a very controlled person.
But as I am Gemini, there are two persons in me.
I was jumping up.
And I said, listen, perhaps you have a mother, or perhaps you had one once.
You ought to be ashamed.
And he was looking at me, and he said, calm down, calm down.
If there is an emergency for your mother, I will help.
And I invented a story on the spur of the moment.
I said the gardener made [INAUDIBLE] at the gestapo that my mother is the aunt of the [INAUDIBLE] murderer, Frankfurter.
That was a man who was shot in Paris, yeah?
By a Jewish guy from [INAUDIBLE] Vienna.
And I knew this story, because the uncle of this guy was our rabbi.
So I knew he was already in Dachau.
And I said, this guy said my mother is the aunt, but I can raise my hand, the story is not true.
Only, I meant another thing, and he meant another thing.
Anyway, my mother got a certificate the next day.
That is the second miracle I told you.
And my mother arrived on her birthday, on the 2nd April, on [INAUDIBLE] Pesach.
That was the second miracle.
And my mother helped me with child and with the household.
And I could work.
My husband was still not very good at work.
But shortly before the war break out, we were lucky.
Our lift arrived from Vienna with the furniture.
With the furniture, and he took-- he found someone with money, an attorney, and he opened some storage place.
And [INAUDIBLE] came all our furniture in.
And we sold our complete household, whatever there was.
There was very costly furniture, antique furniture, and the most beautiful carpets.
And we sold everything.
And that started, you see.
That gave us some money.
And we felt a little bit more secure.
And we needed money, because-- this is, speak of furniture, the only piece I kept, because it was from my grandmother.
That was the only piece.
And everything else was sold.
